title: Firestone Ceramics v. Court of Appeals — Sauce for the Poor Goose, a Postscript
primary_topics: improper en banc takeover of Division case
res judicata via substantial identity of parties
sub_topics: Court en banc 8-7 takeover (CJ Davide, JJ Bellosillo, Kapunan, Mendoza, Purisima, Buena, Santiago, De Leon vs JJ Melo, Puno, Vitug, Panganiban, Quisumbing, Pardo, Reyes)
OCT No. 4216 (1929 CFI Rizal land registration)
burden of proving title nullity (rests with petitioner not respondent)
Sauce for the poor goose / rich gander maxim

stances.claim: The en banc's 8-7 takeover of the Firestone Ceramics case was inappropriate — the movants 'had utterly failed to adduce any legal reason for such referral'; the resulting unanimous denial of Motion for Reconsideration (January 30, 2001) merely 'showed the folly of failing to heed the maxim Sauce for the poor goose should be the same sauce for the rich gander.'
stances.rhetorical_move: structural-critique of unwarranted en banc absorption + aphorism citation

stances.claim: Absolute identity of parties is not required for res judicata — only substantial identity; petitioner Firestone, though not a party in Margolles, raised 'exactly the same argument and relied on the same evidence' to invalidate OCT No. 4216, making them 'privy in law' for res judicata purposes.

The burden of proving the nullity of a 1929 land-registration judgment rests with the petitioner, not the original applicants — 'compliance with this burden should have been proven in the original proceedings instituted by the Gana spouses in 1927, and must be legally presumed to have been satisfied as a decree was issued.'

Annulling 70-year-old Torrens titles only to enable government to dispose lands anew to subsequent private applicants 'would betray a subservience to technicality that will not be in the public interest… This is a simple matter of equity and good sense.'
The banc's takeover 'uselessly and imprudently clogged the Court's dockets' — a wasted exercise of precious time and attention with no doctrinal value added by the en banc's intervention.

institutions: Court of First Instance of Rizal
cases: Firestone Ceramics v. Court of Appeals
Valencia v. RTC of Quezon City (April 3, 1990)
Widows and Orphans Associations v. CA (August 7, 1992)
events: January 30, 2001 unanimous Motion for Reconsideration denial

signature_phrases: Sauce for the poor goose should be the same sauce for the rich gander
a simple matter of equity and good sense
uselessly and imprudently clogged the Court's dockets
register_markers: self-referential cross-citation to prior book chapter
aphoristic-maxim insertion (Sauce for the poor goose)
decision_framework_signals: applies substantial-identity-of-parties doctrine for res judicata (not absolute-identity)
uses burden-of-proof allocation framework (petitioner challenging old title bears burden)
frames equity-and-good-sense as override against technicality-driven annulment
applies 'sauce for the poor goose = sauce for the rich gander' as institutional-equality principle
uses structural-cost critique of unnecessary en banc absorption
cross_references: Chapter 22 of Transparency, Unanimity & Diversity (2000 ed., pp. 413-433)
